Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

Level 2: you have used 54 actions; a strong score needs about 42 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: I'm wasting too many actions. Let me check the current state and try to be much more efficient. I need to understand the movement mechanic better and move blocks in larger jumps.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] not in ['B', 'W', 'c', 'G']:
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']),
                  sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']))
        if center[0] > 5:
            print(f"Node: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, center~({center[0]:.0f},{center[1]:.0f})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

Node: color=p, pixels=9, center~(32,38)
Node: color=M, pixels=4, center~(44,42)
Node: color=M, pixels=4, center~(56,16)

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The block at (44, 42) still didn't move! The click at (48, 30) didn't move it. Maybe the movement mechanic has a range limit, or maybe blocks can only move a certain distance per click.
Let me reconsider. Looking at the previous successful moves:
- Block at (36, 18) moved to (32, 22) when clicked at (32, 22) - distance sqrt(4^2 + 4^2) = sqrt(32) ≈ 5.7
- Block at (32, 22) moved to (28, 26) when clicked at (28, 26) - distance sqrt(4^2 + 4^2) = sqrt(32) ≈ 5.7
- Block at (28, 26) moved to (26, 28) when clicked at (26, 28) - distance sqrt(2^2 + 2^2) = sqrt(8) ≈ 2.8
- Block at (26, 28) moved to (26, 30) when clicked at (26, 30) - distance sqrt(0^2 + 2^2) = 2
- Block at (40, 36) moved to (32, 34) when clicked at (32, 34) - distance sqrt(8^2 + 2^2) = sqrt(68) ≈ 8.2
- Block at (56, 46) moved to (48, 44) when clicked at (48, 44) - distance sqrt(8^2 + 2^2) = sqrt(68) ≈ 8.2
- Block at (48, 44) moved to (44, 42) when clicked at (44, 42) - distance sqrt(4^2 + 2^2) = sqrt(20) ≈ 4.5
So blocks can move up to about 8-9 pixels per click. The click at (48, 30) was distance sqrt(4^2 + 12^2) = sqrt(160) ≈ 12.6 from (44, 42), which is too far.
Let me try clicking closer to (44, 42), like at (38, 36):
Distance from (44, 42) to (38, 36): sqrt(6^2 + 6^2) = sqrt(72) ≈ 8.5
Distance from (32, 38) to (38, 36): sqrt(6^2 + 2^2) = sqrt(40) ≈ 6.3
Distance from (56, 16) to (38, 36): sqrt(18^2 + 20^2) = sqrt(724) ≈ 26.9
Hmm, (32, 38) is closer to (38, 36) than (44, 42) is. So clicking at (38, 36) would move the purple block.
Let me try clicking at (40, 38):
Distance from (44, 42) to (40, 38): sqrt(4^2 + 4^2) = sqrt(32) ≈ 5.7
Distance from (32, 38) to (40, 38): sqrt(8^2 + 0^2) = 8
Distance from (56, 16) to (40, 38): sqrt(16^2 + 22^2) = sqrt(740) ≈ 27.2
Good, (40, 38) is closest to (44, 42). Let me try this.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move magenta block at (44, 42) toward blob: click (40, 38)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 40, 'col': 38}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
